subject: Nikita Kramar
purpose: Compile source-backed NCP signals, likely resonant project ideas, and an obviously fake WWNKD decision toy from Nikita Kramar's public site.
liveWire: Nikita seems most interested in leverage that preserves taste: agents, open web context, proof loops, and human judgment instead of generic automation hype.

machineLayer.name: llms.txt
machineLayer.description: Short agent-readable summary of the joke and boundaries.

machineLayer.name: ncp.json
machineLayer.description: NCP manifest: what the bit is and what files exist.

machineLayer.name: context-pack.json
machineLayer.description: Compact summary for retrieval or handoff.

machineLayer.name: claims.json
machineLayer.description: Signals, ideas, receipts, and WWNKD moves.

signals.title: He wants the web readable, durable, and owned.
signals.body: His homepage is unusually explicit about the Open Web, digital gardens, owning your data, and making public knowledge available by URL. That makes an agent-readable personal-site layer feel adjacent rather than random.

signals.title: His AI taste is context, rules, access, docs, and proof.
signals.body: The strongest technical pattern is not 'use AI.' It is: shape the environment so agents have the context and permissions to do useful work with less human intervention.

signals.title: He treats agent fluency as a separate craft.
signals.body: The Hidden Third Variable essay is the cleanest overlap: agent-tool mastery is not the same as knowing the domain or writing code. It is task shaping, steering, debugging, and knowing when the model is off the rails.

signals.title: The interesting tension is automation versus what life is for.
signals.body: The site pairs one-person leverage, agent stacks, and orchestration with riding, health, Mandarin, travel, music, and cooking. That tension is more interesting than 'full automation good.'

signals.title: He seems to like weird practical artifacts, not just takes.
signals.body: The public log and side projects point toward concrete experiments: agent social networks, prompt injection, web extraction, proof in the real world, Zoomies, and a basic AI chat surface.

ideas.title: NCP: Nikita Context Protocol
ideas.short: The joke is the product: a tiny agent-readable layer for a personal site.
ideas.body: Not a grand standard and not a personality clone. Just a page plus a few files that tell agents: here is what is public, here is what is inferred, here are the unknowns, here are the source links, and here is what you should ask instead of pretending to know.
ideas.opener: I made a dumb thing called NCP, because your site already reads like it wants to be useful to synthetic readers. What should a personal site expose to agents, and what should it absolutely refuse?

ideas.title: Context Autopsy / Rule Doctor
ideas.short: When an agent run fails, classify the failure and write the next rule.
ideas.body: This extends the Hidden Third Variable essay into a concrete artifact. Was the failure bad task shape, missing docs, weak rules, wrong tools, bad retrieval, no eval, or a human-approval boundary? The output is a patch to the environment, not another prompt incantation.
ideas.opener: Your third-variable essay made me think the missing artifact is a failed-run autopsy. What should the report after a bad agent run actually say?

ideas.title: What should agents not touch?
ideas.short: The counterweight to leverage is choosing what stays embodied, slow, or human-approved.
ideas.body: This is not 'use agents to run every hobby.' It is the better question: if agents make execution cheap, where does he want friction, ritual, approval, or direct experience to remain?
ideas.opener: If agents keep taking execution off the table, what parts of life do you preserve on purpose instead of optimizing?

examples.label: Show him this
examples.text: I want to show Nikita an artifact that feels like it came from reading his site, not from pitching him.

examples.label: Agent failed
examples.text: An agent run failed and I need to decide whether the fix is prompt, context, rules, tools, retrieval, or eval.

examples.label: Avoid pitching
examples.text: I have an idea that might resonate, but I do not want to sell him or turn it into a fake framework.

examples.label: Website layer
examples.text: I am building a personal website layer that agents can read without pretending to be the person.

wwnkd.boundary: WWNKD means the ordinary joke version of 'I wonder what he would do here.' It returns candidate moves from public-source patterns, not answers in Nikita's voice.

moves.title: Make it a small working thing.
moves.body: A Nikita-shaped move is probably less manifesto, more artifact. Show the smallest version that does the job, then ask what breaks.
steps: Name the primitive you borrowed from his site.
Make one visible demo, not six speculative branches.
Attach receipts so he can argue with the source, not your vibe.
Ask the question that would improve the artifact.

moves.title: Turn the site into context, not lore.
moves.body: If the object is a website, the move is to make it useful to humans and agents at the same time: readable page, source ledger, compact JSON, and a tiny instruction file.
steps: Keep the public page short enough for a human.
Put the dense receipts in machine-readable files.
Make the main question impossible to miss.

moves.title: Autopsy the failed run.
moves.body: Do not ask whether the agent is smart. Ask what part of the operating environment made the failure likely.
steps: Classify the failure: task, context, rule, tool, retrieval, eval, or approval boundary.
Write the smallest rule or doc patch that would have prevented it.
Replay or simulate the run against that patch.
Keep the receipt so the environment gets better over time.

moves.title: Bring the question, not the pitch.
moves.body: If it starts sounding like a deck, cut it down until it becomes a thing he can poke.
steps: Say what you read on his site that triggered the idea.
Show the artifact in one minute.
Ask where the boundary should be.
Let him rename, reject, or redirect it.

receipts.whyItMatters: This is the source of the NCP joke: a personal site can be written for humans and future scrapers at the same time.
receipts.ask: What should a personal site expose to agents, and what should it refuse?

receipts.claim: Agent work depends on context architecture
receipts.whyItMatters: The useful overlap is not generic AI enthusiasm; it is rules, docs, access, orchestration, and reducing needless intervention.
receipts.ask: When an agent fails, which part of the environment do you inspect first?

receipts.claim: Agent-tool mastery is a separate skill
receipts.whyItMatters: This grounds the Context Autopsy idea in something he actually wrote, rather than in a consultant maturity model.
receipts.ask: What should the report after a failed agent run actually say?

receipts.claim: He is testing one-person leverage with current agent stacks
receipts.whyItMatters: This makes small, working leverage demos more resonant than broad speculation.
receipts.ask: Is one-person leverage a goal, a benchmark, or a stress test?

receipts.claim: Automation is paired with embodied anchors
receipts.whyItMatters: This is the tension to ask about carefully: what gets automated, and what stays lived?
receipts.ask: What parts of life should stay slow or directly experienced?

receipts.claim: His logs collect odd, concrete AI/web/security artifacts
receipts.whyItMatters: The right conversational offering is probably a weird useful object, not an abstract thesis.
receipts.ask: Which weird artifact is actually worth turning into a working tool?
